_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal curve upward sloping as longer maturities carry higher yields reflecting greater risk and time. Today, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.48%, with the 10-year minus 2-year spread (2s10s) at +0.36 percentage points, indicating a positively sloped, or 'steepened', yield curve. This is in contrast to the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted, as the spread was below zero, signaling unusual market conditions. The current positive spread shows the yield curve has moved away from its deepest inversion point of 0.0, suggesting a return to a more normal configuration.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a widely followed recession indicator; an inversion (spread below zero) has historically preceded recessions by 6-18 months. The fact that the spread spent 2022-2024 inverted and has since re-steepened above zero suggests the initial recession signal from the curve may have passed. However, yield curve un-inversions can occur shortly before recessions actually arrive, reducing the definitiveness of the signal. Meanwhile, the New York Fed assigns a 31% probability of recession in the near term, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty despite the current positive slope.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve signals have shifted from a recessionary inversion to a normalized upward slope, while the NY Fed's 31% recession probability indicates moderate but not heightened risk. The re-steepening of the curve, coupled with lingering recession concerns, suggests a complex economic environment without clear imminence of downturn. Investors and analysts should note the curve’s un-inversion caveat and interpret these signals as part of a broader economic assessment.
